title: Rust borrow checker rejects code that looks correct with multiple mutable references
body:
The borrow checker enforces that you can have either one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references, but not both simultaneously. This catches real bugs but surprises newcomers.

```rust
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let first = &v[0]; // immutable borrow
v.push(4);         // ERROR: mutable borrow while immutable borrow exists
println!("{}", first);
```

The fix is to limit the scope of the immutable borrow so it doesn't overlap with the mutable operation:

```rust
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let first = v[0]; // copy the value instead of borrowing
v.push(4);        // fine now
println!("{}", first);
```

This is actually protecting you: `push` may reallocate the Vec's backing memory, which would invalidate the reference `first`. Rust prevents the dangling pointer at compile time.

Rust cargo features are additive — you cannot disable a feature enabled by another dependency
body:
Cargo features are purely additive. If crate A enables feature X on crate C, and crate B also depends on C without feature X, crate C is compiled with feature X enabled for both.

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[dependencies]
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt-multi-thread", "macros"] }

# Optional features for your crate
[features]
default = ["json"]
json = ["dep:serde_json"]
full = ["json", "dep:reqwest"]

[dependencies]
serde_json = { version = "1", optional = true }
reqwest = { version = "0.12", optional = true }
```

```bash
# Build with specific features
cargo build --features "json"
cargo build --no-default-features --features "full"
cargo build --all-features # for testing
```

The gotcha: you cannot write `#[cfg(not(feature = "X"))]` and expect it to work reliably, because any dependency in the tree can enable feature X. Design features as additive capabilities, never as mutually exclusive modes. Use separate crates if you need truly exclusive behavior.

Go modules replace directive for local development and forks
body:
Go modules' `replace` directive lets you point a dependency to a local path or a fork. Forgetting to remove it before pushing causes CI failures.

```go
// go.mod
module myapp

go 1.22

require (
    github.com/some/library v1.2.3
)

// Local development — point to local checkout
replace github.com/some/library => ../library

// Fork — point to your fork
replace github.com/some/library => github.com/myfork/library v1.2.4-patch1
```

```bash
# Common commands
go mod tidy          # clean up go.mod and go.sum
go mod download      # download all dependencies
go mod verify        # verify checksums
go mod vendor        # copy deps into vendor/
go get -u ./...      # update all dependencies
go get github.com/some/library@v1.3.0  # update specific dep
```

Gotcha: `replace` directives in library modules are ignored by consumers. Only the main module's `replace` takes effect. This means libraries cannot force replacements on their users. Use a `.ci-check` or pre-commit hook to flag `replace` directives pointing to local paths before merging.

Rust Dockerfile for minimal production container image
body:
A multi-stage Dockerfile produces a minimal Rust binary without build tools in the final image.

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM rust:1.77-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /app

# Cache dependencies — copy manifests first
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main() {}' > src/main.rs
RUN cargo build --release && rm -rf src

# Build actual application
COPY src/ src/
RUN touch src/main.rs && cargo build --release

# Stage 2: Runtime
FROM debian:bookworm-slim

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y ca-certificates && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/myapp /usr/local/bin/myapp

# Non-root user
RUN useradd -r -s /bin/false appuser
USER appuser

EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["myapp"]
```

```bash
# For even smaller images, use musl for static linking
FROM rust:1.77-slim AS builder
RUN rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
RUN cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl

# Then use scratch or alpine
FROM scratch
COPY --from=builder /app/target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/myapp /myapp
ENTRYPOINT ["/myapp"]
# Final image: ~5-15MB
```

The dependency caching trick (copy Cargo.toml first, fake build, then real build) avoids re-downloading crates on every code change. The musl target produces a fully static binary that runs on `scratch` — no OS needed in the container.
